firstLine: I attended Google Cloud Summit India 2026 alongside 1000+ AI builders
excerpt:
I attended Google Cloud Summit India 2026 at Yashobhoomi, Delhi alongside 1000+ AI builders.

The keynote raised the bar. suchit puri, Prashanth Subrahmanyam and Abirami Sukumaran, Ph.D unpacked the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform and shipped working agents on stage. Live, not slideware.

But the beat that stuck with me was a 1:1 conversation with Romin Irani on Antigravity 2.0 and ADK 2.0. We skipped past "what's new" and got into "why now": the rationale behind a shift this big and what it actually takes for builders to adapt instead of chasing every release. The developer's job is shifting from code-first to AI-first. You stop living in an editor and start directing the agent that writes the code. That is not a tooling update, it is a new way of working.

One session that stood out was a preview of an unreleased AI-powered architecture diagramming tool for Google Cloud. Stenal P Jolly pulled back the curtain on its multi-agent architecture. The premise is hard to argue with if you design systems for a living: we lose hours hand-drawing the same diagrams over and over. An agentic system that generates them is not a nice-to-have. It is overdue.

This year I was not there solo. A few of my Deloitte colleagues came along, Lakshay Sharma, Akshay Goel, SAJAL KULSHRESHTHA, and having people from your own team in the room changes the dynamic. Every session turned into a quick huddle on how we can actually apply this to our work.

Left with a full notebook and a short list I actually intend to act on.

P.S. The second photo was generated live at the event using Nano Banana 2! Pro-grade output at Flash speed, rendered in seconds while you watch.

#GoogleCloudSummitIndia #AgenticAI #GoogleCloudIndia #ADK #Antigravity #GeminiEnterprise

firstLine: I just built a 3D agentic RAG app that makes the entire retrieval process visible
excerpt:
I just built a 3D agentic RAG app using claude sdk and gemini embedding 2 that makes the entire retrieval process visible. Every vector, every score, every decision, as it happens. Not just another prototype or recording. It's live, on my site, for anyone to play around with, guided walkthrough or bring your own key. Link in the comments.

Most RAG is a black box. A question goes in, an answer comes out and the reasoning in between is invisible. That gap is exactly where hallucinations hide.

I gave Claude a hybrid search tool and let it drive. When the retrieved context is thin, the model reformulates its own query and searches again, iterating until the evidence is sufficient. Retrieval becomes a reasoning process. The model decides when it has enough, rather than answering from whatever a single static lookup happened to return.

Underneath, every query resolves through 2 retrieval strategies running in parallel:

• Semantic search ranks by cosine similarity over the embeddings, so "neural net" and "deep learning" converge even with no shared tokens.

• Lexical search uses BM25 to anchor precision: exact names, rare terms, error codes, the signal pure semantics tends to wash out.

The 2 rankings are then merged through Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), which blends both into a single ordered set without either one dominating.

All 8 stages, rendered in 3D vector space:
Chunk → Embed → Store → Embed Query → Retrieve → Fuse → Augment → Grounded Answer

Watch the retrieval unfold below.

#RAG #AgenticAI #Anthropic #Claude #VoyageAI #Google #Gemini

firstLine: I gave my website an employee. It works 2 days a week and never asks questions.
excerpt:
My website has an employee now. It works 2 days a week, never asks questions and only emails me when something actually matters.

It's an ambient agent. Most AI agents are reactive: they wait for a prompt, answer and go quiet. Ambient agents invert that, running on their own schedule or in response to events instead of waiting on a human. From there it's a design decision. I built mine to stay quiet and surface only what crosses a threshold worth my attention.

Mine wakes up every Monday and Thursday, looks at the week, and sends me one short email. Traffic trends, what visitors asked my site's ai agent, and where it fell short on answers. That's it. No dashboard to open. It doesn't just report, it reasons over the week and comes back with what's working and what's worth improving.

firstLine: 80 made it to the Google Cloud Agentic Premier League Grand Finale. 15 finished on top. I was one of them.
excerpt:
3,000+ builders entered across 30 cities in India. 80 made it to the Grand Finale. 15 finished at the top. I'm one of them. Easily one of the sharpest rooms full of AI builders I've been in this year.

The Google Cloud Agentic Premier League Grand Finale took place at Google Ananta in Bengaluru. One time-boxed agentic challenge followed by a live pitch to Googlers.

The challenge focused on resolving logistical chaos and security gaps at crowded cricket stadiums. I built a real-time multi-agent command platform that dynamically routes fan flow and automates emergency responses using Antigravity 2.0 and Gemini 3.5 Flash.

firstLine: I spent a weekend inside Google's PromptWars and the Claude community — here's what I saw.
excerpt: I spent the last 2 days in 2 different rooms with 100+ Builders pushing the absolute limits of AI. Day 1 was PromptWars at the Google office — I built a fully functional travel planning app from scratch, vibe coded with Antigravity, orchestrating Gemini and Claude in a few hours. I landed in the top 15. Day 2 was meeting the fastest-growing community of AI builders in the Claude ecosystem. Two communities. Two days. One very clear signal: things are moving fast.

firstLine: Unleashing the power of Claude Code Sub-agents
excerpt: I discovered a single question rule for Claude Code subagents: does the intermediate work matter? No? Delegate. Yes? Keep it in the main thread. Code reviews are where this gets interesting.

firstLine: Context bloat is quietly wrecking your coding agent.
excerpt: Context bloat is quietly wrecking your coding agent. Context engineering is how you fix it. Here's the mental model I use to keep agent context clean across long sessions.

Every developer building AI agents today hits the same 2 walls: fragmented tooling and a coding agent that hallucinates. I showed how agents-cli collapses both in a single workflow at Google Cloud Next '26.

firstLine: The biggest blind spot in AI for incident response is simple: it does not know what your team already learned.
excerpt: On-call engineers keep getting sent down paths their teammates already ruled out last month. I built a multi-agent system that fixes that — starting with your team's own runbooks and past incidents.

firstLine: I vibe coded a GCP Advisor agent that never guesses.
excerpt: Vibe coding gets you a prototype fast, but it cuts corners — hallucinated APIs, invented patterns. ADK agent skills fix that by giving Gemini CLI the right context to build correctly from architecture through evals.

firstLine: I built an agent that reads every GCP release note so you don't have to.
excerpt: Meet ReleaseLens: an AI agent that monitors Google Cloud release notes, filters the noise, and tells developers exactly how updates impact their specific tech stacks. No more changelog spelunking.
